worth_it_explanation: PPS let a publisher pass first-party audience and contextual data to bidders without sharing user identifiers, which can raise the value of programmatic impressions as third-party cookies and shared IDs decline. The check is a review of the signals a publisher already sends through GPT and configures in GAM, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time. The finding points to a concrete configuration the publisher can correct, either turning on signals that are missing or fixing signals encoded in a form bidders cannot read.
intro: Publisher Provided Signals (PPS) are first-party audience and contextual signals a publisher passes to bidders at the time of the ad request, sent as standardized taxonomy category IDs rather than as user identifiers. On a website, those signals reach the auction through the Google Publisher Tag (GPT) ad request and the mappings configured in Google Ad Manager (GAM). This subcategory is about whether those signals are configured at all and whether they use the supported taxonomies correctly, rather than about how the demand they bring in is priced. These checks confirm that a publisher's first-party data is reaching the bidders who can act on it, and that it arrives in a form bidders can interpret.

audit_checks.title: Publisher Provided Signals are not configured, so first-party data never reaches bidders
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When Publisher Provided Signals are not configured, a publisher's first-party audience and contextual data never reaches the auction, so bidders price the impression with less information than the publisher already holds. As shared identifiers decline, the signals a publisher can pass without sharing user data become one of the few ways to communicate audience value, and leaving them off forgoes that demand on every impression.

educate.base:
Publisher Provided Signals (PPS) are a way for a publisher to send first-party audience and contextual data to bidders at the time of the ad request, sent as standardized taxonomy category IDs rather than as user identifiers. On a website, those signals travel through the Google Publisher Tag (GPT) ad request once a publisher has set up the mappings in Google Ad Manager (GAM) and turned on sharing with demand channels. The point of PPS is that a publisher already knows things about its audience and its pages, and PPS lets that knowledge reach the auction so bidders can value the impression on it.

The value of this has grown as the shared identifiers bidders once relied on have declined. Third-party cookies and cross-site identifiers carried much of the audience signal in the open auction, and as they thin out, a publisher's own first-party data becomes one of the few remaining ways to tell a bidder who the audience is and what the page is about. PPS carries that data without exposing a user identifier, so it works inside the privacy constraints that pushed the identifiers out in the first place.

When PPS is not configured, none of that reaches the auction. Bidders see the ad request, but they do not see the audience or contextual signals the publisher could have sent, so they price the impression on whatever they can infer on their own. The impression still sells, but it sells without the publisher's first-party data attached, which removes a lever the publisher controls.

A few signs point to PPS not being configured:
  • No publisher provided signals mappings exist under 'Signals' in Google Ad Manager, so no audience or contextual segments are defined to send
  • Demand channel settings do not have publisher provided signals enabled for any demand channel
  • The GPT ad request carries no taxonomy signals, which the Google Publisher Console shows when you inspect the page request
You may want to confirm whether PPS is set up and reaching demand channels. One approach is to check for signal mappings in Google Ad Manager, confirm sharing is enabled in demand channel settings, and inspect a live ad request in the Google Publisher Console to see whether signals are present.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site running GPT, the first-party data worth sending through PPS is usually content category and audience interest. Editorial pages map cleanly to contextual segments, and registered or logged-in readers carry audience signals. You may want to confirm that the content and audience data the site already holds is mapped and sent, so bidders can value editorial inventory on what the publisher knows about the page and the reader.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site running GPT, product and category pages carry strong contextual signal and shoppers carry audience signal, both of which PPS can pass without sharing a user identifier. You may want to confirm that the product and category context, along with any first-party shopper audiences, is mapped and sent, so bidders can price the higher-intent commercial inventory on the publisher's first-party data rather than on inference alone.

learn_more.source_title: Share publisher provided signals at time of ad request (Beta)

investigate.intro: Review whether Publisher Provided Signals are mapped in Google Ad Manager and enabled for demand channels, then inspect a live GPT ad request to confirm signals are actually present.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Signals', then 'Publisher provided signals', and note whether any signal mappings exist for IAB Audience Taxonomy, IAB Content Taxonomy, or structured signals.

Open 'Demand channel settings', then the 'Default settings' tab, and record whether publisher provided signals are enabled for Google demand and for any Authorized Buyers, Open Bidding, or SDK Bidding partners.
Record whether mappings exist but sharing is off, sharing is on but no mappings exist, or neither is configured.

gpt_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Open a live page that runs Google Publisher Tag (GPT) and open the Google Publisher Console, either by appending '?google_console=1' to the page URL or by running 'googletag.openConsole()' in the browser's JavaScript console.
Open the 'Page request' tab and inspect the ad request to check whether publisher provided signals are present in the request sent to Google Ad Manager.
Record whether the ad request carries taxonomy signals or whether no signals are attached, which confirms PPS is not reaching the request from the page.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether publisher provided signals mappings exist in Google Ad Manager, whether sharing is enabled for the relevant demand channels, and whether a live GPT ad request carries signals. A setup with mappings defined, sharing enabled, and signals present in the ad request passes. A setup with no mappings, sharing left off, or an ad request that carries no signals is a fail recorded with which part of the configuration is missing.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether signal mappings exist, whether demand channel sharing is enabled, and whether a live ad request carries signals above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not have Publisher Provided Signals (PPS) configured, so the site's first-party audience and contextual data never reaches bidders in the ad request. PPS lets a publisher pass that data through the Google Publisher Tag (GPT) request as standardized taxonomy IDs without sharing a user identifier, and as shared identifiers decline it has become one of the few ways to communicate audience value to the auction. {{voice_recommend}} mapping the site's audience and content data to the supported taxonomies in Google Ad Manager, enabling sharing in demand channel settings, and confirming with the Google Publisher Console that live ad requests carry the signals. After the change, recheck a live ad request to confirm the signals are present and reaching demand.

audit_checks.title: Signals use ad hoc values instead of the supported taxonomies bidders can interpret
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Publisher Provided Signals only carry value to a bidder when the bidder can interpret them, which means the signals have to use the supported industry taxonomies rather than a publisher's internal labels. Audience and content data left as ad hoc key-values that were never mapped to a taxonomy reaches the auction in a form bidders cannot read, so the first-party data is sent but not understood, and the demand it should have brought in never materializes.

educate.base:
Publisher Provided Signals (PPS) only help a bidder if the bidder can interpret them, and interpretation depends on a shared vocabulary. A publisher's internal labels, such as a custom key-value of 'section=fishing-gear' or an audience named 'loyal-readers', mean something inside that publisher's setup, but a bidder has no way to know what they map to. Industry taxonomies solve this by giving every party the same category names, so a contextual or audience signal sent as a taxonomy category ID means the same thing to the publisher and to every bidder that receives it.

Google Ad Manager (GAM) supports a specific set of taxonomies for PPS. Audience signals map to the IAB Audience Taxonomy, and contextual signals map to the IAB Content Taxonomy, with Google's own structured signals available for additional data that those taxonomies do not cover. In Google Ad Manager, a publisher maps its first-party audiences, custom key-values, or content management system (CMS) key-values to the matching taxonomy categories, and from then on the ad request carries the standardized category IDs rather than the publisher's internal strings.

The failure here is subtle because the signals can look configured while still being unreadable. A publisher may have first-party audiences and rich key-values flowing into the ad request, yet if those values were never mapped to a supported taxonomy, they arrive as ad hoc strings that bidders cannot match to anything. The data is sent and the request is heavier, yet none of it lands because the bidder receives labels it cannot interpret. Mapping the same data to the IAB taxonomies turns those labels into categories the whole demand side already understands.

A few signs point to signals that are not mapped to the supported taxonomies:
  • Audiences or key-values are sent as raw internal values with no mapping to the IAB Audience Taxonomy or IAB Content Taxonomy recorded in Google Ad Manager
  • The taxonomy version in use does not match what Google Ad Manager supports for PPS, so the category IDs do not resolve
  • The signal mappings cover only a small share of the site's content or audiences, leaving most pages and segments with no taxonomy signal
You may want to confirm that the signals being sent are mapped to the supported taxonomies rather than passed as internal labels. One approach is to review the taxonomy mappings in Google Ad Manager against the audiences and key-values the site actually uses, and confirm the versions match the IAB Audience Taxonomy and IAB Content Taxonomy that Google Ad Manager supports.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, content sections and topics are a natural fit for the IAB Content Taxonomy, and registered-reader segments map to the IAB Audience Taxonomy. You may want to confirm that the site's section and topic labels are mapped to content taxonomy categories rather than sent as raw internal section names, so the contextual signal on editorial pages is one bidders can read.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages carry strong contextual meaning that maps to the IAB Content Taxonomy, and shopper segments map to the IAB Audience Taxonomy. You may want to confirm that product category labels and shopper audiences are mapped to the supported taxonomy categories rather than passed as internal catalog values, so the higher-intent commercial pages send a contextual signal the demand side can interpret.

learn_more.source_title: Map key-values and audience segments to taxonomies (Beta)
Share publisher provided signals at time of ad request (Beta)

investigate.intro: Review the signal mappings in Google Ad Manager to see whether audiences and key-values are mapped to the supported taxonomies and at the correct versions, rather than sent as raw internal values.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Signals', then 'Publisher provided signals', and review the signal type cards for IAB Audience Taxonomy, IAB Content Taxonomy, and structured signals.
For each card, open 'View or create mappings' and record which first-party audiences, custom key-values, or CMS key-values are mapped to taxonomy categories and which are not.
Confirm the taxonomy versions in use match what Google Ad Manager supports for PPS, and note any audiences or key-values that are sent but never mapped to a supported taxonomy.

gpt_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Open a live page that runs Google Publisher Tag (GPT) and open the Google Publisher Console by appending '?google_console=1' to the URL or running 'googletag.openConsole()' in the browser's JavaScript console.
Open the 'Page request' tab and inspect the ad request to confirm the signals present are sent as standardized taxonomy category IDs rather than as raw internal strings.
Record whether the signals in the request resolve to taxonomy categories or whether they appear as ad hoc values that were never mapped.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records which audiences and key-values are mapped to the IAB Audience Taxonomy and IAB Content Taxonomy in Google Ad Manager, whether the versions match what Google Ad Manager supports, and whether a live ad request carries taxonomy category IDs rather than raw values. Signals mapped to the supported taxonomies at the correct versions and covering the site's main content and audiences pass. Signals sent as ad hoc internal values, mapped at an unsupported version, or covering only a small share of the inventory are a fail recorded with the unmapped audiences or key-values.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which audiences and key-values are mapped to the supported taxonomies, the versions in use, and whether a live request carries taxonomy IDs above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} sends Publisher Provided Signals (PPS) as ad hoc internal values rather than mapping them to the supported industry taxonomies, so bidders receive labels they cannot interpret. PPS only carries value when the audience and content signals are mapped to the IAB Audience Taxonomy and IAB Content Taxonomy that Google Ad Manager (GAM) supports. Those taxonomies give the publisher and every bidder the same category vocabulary. {{voice_recommend}} mapping the site's audiences and key-values to the supported taxonomies in Google Ad Manager, confirming the versions match what Google Ad Manager supports, and verifying with the Google Publisher Console that the ad request carries standardized taxonomy IDs. After the change, recheck a live ad request to confirm the signals resolve to taxonomy categories rather than raw values.
